symbols: mandorla (diamond shape within oval) — the divine glory surrounding Christ in his heavenly state
open gospel book — Christ as the living Word and teacher of truth
blessing hand raised — the authority to bless, judge, and save
gold cruciform halo — divinity and the cross as the instrument of salvation
white and gold robes — purity, divinity, and heavenly glory
red corners — the four evangelists or the four corners of creation over which Christ reigns
enthroned posture — Christ as universal king and judge
description: A large Orthodox icon depicting Christ seated on a throne within a dark oval mandorla set against a red background with red angular forms at the four corners. Christ wears white-gold robes and has a gold cruciform halo. He holds an open book with text in his left hand and raises his right hand in blessing. His face is solemn and frontal with a dark beard and shoulder-length hair. The overall composition is symmetrical and hieratic, with the mandorla creating concentric zones of divine glory.
theological_description: This icon of Christ in Majesty — the Pantocrator, 'ruler of all' — presents the eschatological Christ who will come again in glory to judge the living and the dead. The mandorla, the almond-shaped glory that surrounds Christ, indicates his divine nature transcending the created order. In Orthodox theology, this icon type is placed in the central dome of the church, gazing down upon the worshippers as the all-seeing, all-ruling Lord. The open gospel book contains the words of Christ — 'I am the way, the truth, and the life' (John 14:6) or similar text — establishing him as the incarnate Logos, the Word through whom all things were made (John 1:3). The blessing hand uses the arrangement of fingers that in Orthodox tradition spells the Christogram IC XC (Jesus Christ). The red background and angular forms evoke the cherubim and seraphim described in Ezekiel's vision (Ezekiel 1) and Isaiah's temple vision (Isaiah 6). Rublev's icon presents Christ not merely as a historical figure but as the eternal Logos — the one who was, who is, and who is to come (Revelation 1:8).

scripture_refs.reason: The Alpha and Omega, who is and was and is to come, the Almighty — the theological identity this icon proclaims
I am the way, the truth, and the life — the probable text on the open gospel book
In the beginning was the Word — Christ as the eternal Logos depicted in cosmic majesty
The Son of Man coming in glory on his throne — the eschatological dimension of the Pantocrator
The figure on the throne surrounded by radiance like a rainbow — Ezekiel's vision informing the mandorla tradition
